sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Intramuscular injection delivers medication into muscle for systemic absorption when it is the preferred route or when IV access is unnecessary, unavailable, or would delay treatment.

sections.title: Injection Sites
sections.content:
**Deltoid:**
- Suitable for smaller adult IM volumes when muscle bulk is adequate

**Vastus lateralis:**
- Preferred for adrenaline in anaphylaxis and for many paediatric IM injections

**Ventrogluteal:**
- May be appropriate in adults when local practice, training, and drug choice support its use

Select the site based on the medication, expected volume, patient size, and urgency

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Confirm the medication, dose, patient, route, timing, and indication

2. Select the most appropriate site, needle, and syringe for the patient and medication

3. Prepare the medication aseptically and clean the skin if indicated by local practice

4. Insert the needle into the chosen muscle at the correct angle for the site and patient habitus

5. Inject the medication steadily, withdraw the needle safely, and apply light pressure if needed

6. Dispose of sharps immediately and monitor for effect and adverse reactions

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Poor site selection or inadequate needle length can turn an IM dose into a subcutaneous dose and delay absorption
- The anterolateral thigh remains the preferred site for adrenaline in anaphylaxis
- Reassess for both therapeutic effect and complications after administration
- Document the drug, dose, route, site, time, and response
